heroOverview: Sialolithiasis is the formation of calcified mineral deposits — salivary stones — inside the ducts or parenchyma of the salivary glands, most often the submandibular gland, which accounts for roughly 80–90% of all cases. The condition typically presents as a painful, recurring swelling beneath the jaw or in the cheek that flares at mealtimes when saliva production peaks, then gradually subsides. Salivary stones affect an estimated 1 in 10,000–30,000 people per year, with the peak incidence in adults aged 30–60. Most stones are small enough to pass or be removed without surgery, but untreated obstruction can lead to bacterial sialadenitis and, in rare cases, permanent gland damage. Modern minimally invasive techniques, including endoscopic removal through the duct (sialendoscopy), have largely replaced open surgical excision for the majority of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1–2 cases per 10,000–30,000 people per year; submandibular gland affected in 80–90% of cases
keyStats.demographics: Most common in adults aged 30–60; slight male predominance reported in several series
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in the fourth and fifth decades of life
keyStats.globalCases: Prevalence in autopsy series estimated at 1.2% of the general population

definition: Sialolithiasis (ICD-10: K11.5) is the presence of one or more calcified concretions within the salivary ductal system or gland tissue. Stones form when organic matter — shed epithelial cells, mucoproteins, and bacteria — becomes a scaffold for the sequential deposition of calcium phosphate and calcium carbonate, ultimately creating a hard, white or yellowish calculus. The submandibular gland is most vulnerable because its duct (Wharton's duct) is long, runs upward against gravity, and carries saliva that is more alkaline and richer in calcium and mucins compared to parotid secretions. The parotid gland accounts for about 10–20% of stones, and the sublingual and minor salivary glands are rarely affected. Stones range from a millimetre or less to over 2 cm; most are solitary, though multiple stones occur in 20–25% of cases. When a stone partially or completely obstructs the duct, saliva backs up and the gland swells, typically peaking at meals. Chronic obstruction promotes ascending bacterial infection (sialadenitis), which can cause the gland to fill with pus. Sialolithiasis falls under the scope of gastroenterology, oral and maxillofacial surgery, and ear, nose and throat surgery.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, affecting Wharton's duct or the submandibular gland itself; stones are often large and radio-opaque, causing meal-related swelling beneath the mandible.
Accounts for 10–20% of cases; stones form in Stensen's duct and present as cheek swelling in front of the ear at mealtimes; parotid stones are less radio-opaque than submandibular ones.
Rare; small stones in the floor of the mouth or minor salivary glands may appear as firm, palpable nodules and are often managed with simple intraoral incision.
Multiple episodes of obstruction and infection with or without radiographically visible stones; often associated with ductal strictures that perpetuate stasis.

primarySymptoms: Acute, painful swelling of the affected salivary gland — submandibular, parotid, or sublingual — that typically begins within minutes of starting a meal and peaks over 30–60 minutes before slowly subsiding.

Mealtime colic: a sharp, cramping pain beneath the jaw or in the cheek triggered by the surge of saliva when food is seen or smelled, a pattern so characteristic it is sometimes called salivary colic.
A firm, tender lump palpable beneath the mandible, in the floor of the mouth, or in front of the ear, corresponding to the obstructed and distended gland.
Reduced or absent saliva flow from the affected duct opening (papilla) on the floor of the mouth or inner cheek, which may be noticed as excessive dryness on one side only.
A small, hard, whitish or yellowish stone visible or palpable at the duct opening in the floor of the mouth in cases where the calculus has migrated near the papilla.
Persistent or worsening swelling that does not fully resolve between meals, suggesting incomplete obstruction or early sialadenitis superimposed on the stone.
Fever, increasing pain, redness, and warmth overlying the gland when secondary bacterial infection (acute sialadenitis) has developed.
Purulent discharge from the duct opening, visible as thick, creamy saliva or frank pus expressed from the papilla, indicating gland infection.
Difficulty opening the mouth fully (trismus) in severe acute sialadenitis, particularly when the parotid or masseteric region is involved.
Dry mouth (xerostomia) on the affected side as chronic obstruction progressively reduces gland function.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild, transient swelling beneath the jaw or in the cheek that appears only at mealtimes and resolves completely within an hour.
A vague sense of pressure or fullness on one side of the mouth floor when eating, without visible swelling.
Slightly reduced saliva flow noticed as intermittent dryness on one side of the mouth.
A tiny hard nodule palpable with the tongue in the floor of the mouth near the lower front teeth.
emergencySigns: Rapidly spreading swelling of the neck or floor of the mouth with difficulty swallowing, speaking, or breathing, suggesting Ludwig's angina — a life-threatening deep-space infection.
Fever above 38.5°C with rigors and a diffusely tender, red, fluctuant gland, indicating abscess formation requiring urgent drainage.
Trismus severe enough to prevent eating or drinking, combined with visible swelling of the parotid or submandibular region.
Skin overlying the gland that is hot, red, and shiny with an area of fluctuance or skin breakdown, suggesting an abscess pointing to the surface.

causes.cause: Salivary stasis and reduced flow rate
causes.description: When saliva moves slowly through the duct — due to dehydration, low oral intake, or ductal anatomy — calcium salts have more time to precipitate out of solution and aggregate around organic nuclei. Stasis is the single most consistent predisposing condition across all published case series.

Hypercalcaemia from any cause (primary hyperparathyroidism, vitamin D excess, malignancy) raises the calcium load filtered into saliva, favouring crystallisation. Even within the normal calcium range, local supersaturation at the duct wall can initiate stone nucleation.

causes.cause: Ductal anatomy of the submandibular gland
causes.description: Wharton's duct is approximately 5 cm long, runs upward at roughly 45 degrees against gravity, and empties into the floor of the mouth — a course that creates natural sites of relative stasis where calcium deposits begin. This anatomical disadvantage explains the strong predilection for submandibular involvement.

causes.cause: Altered salivary composition (alkaline pH and high mucin content)
causes.description: Submandibular saliva is more alkaline (higher pH) than parotid saliva and contains more calcium and mucoproteins. Alkaline conditions reduce the solubility of calcium phosphate and provide a richer organic scaffold for crystallisation.

causes.cause: Ductal injury, stricture, and retrograde bacterial contamination
causes.description: Previous inflammation, trauma, or instrumentation of the duct can leave scar tissue that narrows the lumen, creating upstream stasis and a nidus for stone formation. Retrograde entry of oral bacteria adds an organic core around which mineral layers accumulate.

Inadequate fluid intake concentrates saliva, raising mineral saturation. Anticholinergic drugs (antihistamines, tricyclic antidepressants, bladder agents), diuretics, and other medications that reduce saliva volume promote the same effect, explaining the higher frequency of sialolithiasis in older adults taking polypharmacy.

riskFactors.factor: Dehydration and low daily fluid intake
riskFactors.description: Insufficient hydration concentrates salivary calcium and slows ductal flow, the two factors most directly promoting stone nucleation. Increasing fluid intake is the most straightforward modifiable risk to address.
Anticholinergics, antihistamines, antidepressants, diuretics, and antihypertensives reduce saliva production by up to 40%, creating the salivary stasis that precedes stone formation.
Most large series show a slight male predominance, with a male-to-female ratio of approximately 2:1, possibly related to differences in salivary composition and ductal diameter.
Sialolithiasis is uncommon in children and peaks in the middle decades of adult life, a pattern attributed to cumulative ductal changes and medication use.

Primary hyperparathyroidism and other conditions elevating serum calcium increase the calcium concentration in saliva, raising crystallisation risk; treating the underlying cause can reduce recurrence.
Elevated uric acid levels have been associated with salivary stones in some series, though the mechanism is less well established than for calcium-based stones.
Inflammation and scarring narrow the duct and create sites of stasis where new stones can form, explaining the tendency for recurrence after the first episode.
Tobacco use alters salivary composition and reduces flow, both conducive to stone formation; cessation is part of standard preventive advice.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 30–60 years, who represent the majority of cases
Men, who are affected roughly twice as often as women in most published series
People with chronic dehydration, high medication burden, or gout
Patients with primary hyperparathyroidism or other causes of hypercalcaemia
Older adults on multiple medications that reduce salivary flow
Rarely, children — in whom the condition is unusual and often prompts investigation for metabolic disease
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis typically begins at the bedside. A history of recurrent meal-related swelling of a single major salivary gland combined with a palpable hard mass in the floor of the mouth or over the gland is often enough to make the provisional diagnosis. The clinician inspects and bimanually palpates the floor of the mouth to feel for a stone and assesses whether the duct orifice is red, indurated, or draining pus. Plain X-ray (occlusal or panoramic radiograph) identifies radio-opaque stones, which represent roughly 80–90% of submandibular calculi but only about 40–60% of parotid stones. Ultrasound has become the preferred first-line imaging tool — it is radiation-free, widely available, detects both radio-opaque and radiolucent stones larger than 1–2 mm, and assesses the surrounding gland parenchyma for signs of chronic damage or abscess. CT scan with contrast is reserved for complex cases, recurrence assessment, or when deep-lobe involvement, abscess, or malignancy is a concern; it provides precise stone size and location to guide surgical planning. MRI sialography and conventional sialography are used in specialist centres to map the entire ductal system before endoscopic intervention. Sialendoscopy — direct visual examination of the duct under local anaesthesia — is both diagnostic and therapeutic, allowing simultaneous stone retrieval in many cases. Blood tests are not routinely needed but serum calcium, phosphate, and parathyroid hormone should be checked when multiple or recurrent stones occur to exclude metabolic disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies a palpable stone in the floor of the mouth or over the gland and assesses duct patency as the simplest, most immediate diagnostic step.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The doctor places one finger inside your mouth and one on the outside of your jaw, gently pressing to feel for a hard mass along the course of the duct; the examination takes a few minutes and is mildly uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects stones as small as 1–2 mm regardless of their mineral composition and evaluates gland parenchyma for inflammation or abscess; it is the preferred first-line imaging test.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld probe coated in gel is moved gently over your jaw and neck for 10–15 minutes; it is painless and uses no radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies radio-opaque calcium stones in the submandibular region quickly and cheaply; approximately 80–90% of submandibular stones are visible on plain films.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You bite on a small X-ray film holder while an image is taken; the procedure takes under five minutes and involves a tiny radiation dose.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Precisely localises the stone and its relationship to the duct, detects radiolucent stones missed by X-ray, and assesses for abscess or malignancy in complex cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a scanner for several minutes; intravenous contrast may be given; radiation dose is low but higher than a plain X-ray.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visual examination of the ductal lumen using a miniature endoscope under local anaesthesia; diagnostic and often simultaneously therapeutic, allowing stone fragmentation and retrieval.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fine wire-guided endoscope (0.8–1.6 mm diameter) is advanced through the natural duct opening under local anaesthesia; the procedure takes 30–90 minutes in an outpatient or day-surgery setting.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum calcium, phosphate, and parathyroid hormone
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for metabolic causes — particularly primary hyperparathyroidism — in patients with multiple, bilateral, or recurrent stones.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within a few hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment aims to remove the stone, relieve obstruction, and preserve gland function, and the approach is determined by stone size, location, and whether infection is present. Very small stones (under about 3 mm) near the duct orifice sometimes pass spontaneously with conservative measures: increasing fluid intake to 2–3 litres daily, sucking on sour candy or lemon drops to stimulate saliva flow, gently massaging the gland from back to front, and applying warm compresses. When conservative measures fail, minimally invasive procedural options have replaced open surgery as the standard of care in most specialist centres. Sialendoscopy — endoscopic examination of the duct — allows direct stone removal with baskets or forceps, laser lithotripsy of larger stones, and simultaneous treatment of ductal strictures; published series report stone removal or fragmentation in 70–90% of cases. Extracorporeal shock-wave lithotripsy (ESWL) fragments stones non-invasively using focused acoustic waves, with residual fragments then passing or being washed out endoscopically; parotid stones respond particularly well. Intraoral incision and milking of the stone under local anaesthesia remains a quick, effective technique when the stone is palpable in the floor of the mouth within about 2 cm of the papilla. When acute bacterial sialadenitis complicates the obstruction, antibiotics active against oral streptococci and Staphylococcus aureus (typically co-amoxiclav or clindamycin) are started urgently, with drainage of any abscess. Gland excision (submandibulectomy or parotidectomy) is now reserved for cases with chronic, non-functioning glands, or where minimally invasive options have failed or are not available; the procedure carries a small risk of injury to adjacent nerves.

medicalTreatments.name: Conservative sialogue measures
medicalTreatments.description: Aggressive hydration (2–3 litres daily), sour candy or lemon drops to stimulate saliva flow, gentle gland massage, and warm compresses are first-line for small accessible stones. These measures increase ductal flushing pressure and may dislodge stones near the papilla.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reported spontaneous passage rates of approximately 15–25% for stones under 3 mm with conservative management.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for superimposed sialadenitis
medicalTreatments.description: When bacterial infection accompanies obstruction, oral co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin-clavulanate) for 5–7 days is the standard first-line choice in adults; clindamycin or a macrolide is used in penicillin allergy. Intravenous antibiotics are required for severe infection, abscess, or systemic illness.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical resolution of acute bacterial sialadenitis in approximately 85–90% of cases when combined with removal of the obstructing stone.

medicalTreatments.name: Sialendoscopy (endoscopic stone removal)
medicalTreatments.description: A miniature rigid or semi-rigid endoscope is advanced through the natural duct opening under local anaesthesia; stones are captured with a wire basket, fragmented with a laser, or flushed out. Ductal strictures are simultaneously dilated. The technique avoids external incisions and preserves gland function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial stone removal in 70–90% of appropriately selected cases in published series; success rates are highest for stones under 8–10 mm.

medicalTreatments.name: Extracorporeal shock-wave lithotripsy (ESWL)
medicalTreatments.description: Focused shock waves generated outside the body fragment the stone into small pieces that can then pass spontaneously or be removed endoscopically. Most effective for single parotid stones and submandibular stones under about 7 mm that are away from the duct orifice.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stone fragmentation achieved in roughly 50–70% of cases; symptom-free rates of about 40–60% at two years in parotid stone series.

medicalTreatments.name: Intraoral incision and stone extraction
medicalTreatments.description: Under local anaesthesia, the duct mucosa overlying a palpable distal stone is incised, the stone expressed, and the duct left open or loosely sutured. A simple, fast procedure for stones in the anterior floor of the mouth.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Success rate of approximately 85–95% for accessible distal submandibular duct stones in experienced hands.

surgicalOptions.name: Submandibulectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the submandibular gland through a neck incision; reserved for chronically diseased, non-functioning glands, failed endoscopic attempts, or suspicion of malignancy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effectively resolves sialolithiasis in virtually 100% of cases; main risks are temporary (up to 20%) or permanent (<2%) marginal mandibular and hypoglossal nerve injury.

surgicalOptions.name: Superficial parotidectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the superficial lobe of the parotid gland for large, impacted, or proximally located parotid stones where sialendoscopy has failed; the facial nerve is carefully preserved throughout.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive in eliminating parotid stones; facial nerve weakness occurs temporarily in approximately 15–25% of cases and permanently in under 5% in experienced hands.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sialagogue stimulation with sour foods
alternativeTreatments.description: Sucking lemon drops, sugar-free sour candy, or vitamin C tablets several times daily maximises saliva production and ductal flushing pressure, potentially dislodging small stones and reducing stasis between procedures.

alternativeTreatments.name: Gland massage
alternativeTreatments.description: Gentle bimanual massage of the affected gland toward the duct opening, performed daily, helps mobilise stagnant saliva and small fragments after lithotripsy, complementing formal treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary sodium reduction and adequate hydration
alternativeTreatments.description: Reducing dietary sodium lowers urinary and salivary calcium excretion; combined with high fluid intake this addresses two of the modifiable factors most directly linked to stone formation and recurrence.

whySeeSpecialist: Primary care is appropriate for the initial assessment and management of acute sialadenitis with antibiotics, but a specialist — oral and maxillofacial surgeon, ENT surgeon, or gastroenterologist with salivary gland expertise — should be involved when symptoms recur, when the stone is not accessible to simple palpation and intraoral techniques, or when imaging suggests a deep-lobe location. Sialendoscopy and ESWL require specialist training and equipment. Any unexplained mass within or adjacent to a salivary gland, or a stone that does not follow the typical pattern of meal-related swelling, warrants specialist evaluation to exclude neoplasm.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an oral and maxillofacial surgeon or ENT surgeon with specific experience in sialendoscopy and salivary gland lithotripsy; ask how many sialendoscopies the surgeon performs per year and whether sialendoscopy and ESWL are available in-house. A centre that can offer combined endoscopic and shock-wave approaches will achieve the best outcomes with the least tissue disruption. If hypercalcaemia is found, endocrine involvement to address the metabolic cause is important before any intervention.
preventionStrategies: Drink 2–3 litres of fluid daily to keep saliva well-diluted and maintain strong ductal flow.
Stimulate saliva regularly with sugar-free sour candy, lemon drops, or chewing gum, particularly if you take medications that dry the mouth.
Review any medications that reduce saliva production — antihistamines, antidepressants, diuretics — with your prescriber to see whether alternatives exist.
Have serum calcium checked if you have had multiple or bilateral stones, and treat hypercalcaemia at its source.
Avoid dehydrating habits such as excessive caffeine and alcohol intake, and increase fluid intake during illness or hot weather.
Stop smoking; tobacco reduces salivary flow and alters composition in ways that promote stone nucleation.
lifestyleModifications: Carry a water bottle and sip regularly throughout the day rather than drinking large amounts infrequently.
Eat meals unhurriedly; the sustained salivary flow during a relaxed meal provides better duct flushing than rapid eating.
Perform gentle gland massage toward the mouth after meals if you have a history of sialolithiasis.
Maintain good oral hygiene to reduce the retrograde bacterial contamination that contributes to stone nidus formation.
Manage gout with appropriate medication and dietary changes if hyperuricaemia is a contributing factor.
Stay especially well-hydrated during hot weather, febrile illness, or any period of increased fluid losses.

recommended: Adequate daily water intake — at least 2 litres — to dilute salivary minerals and maintain ductal flushing
Citrus fruits and sugar-free sour foods that stimulate saliva production at mealtimes
A diet moderate in calcium-rich foods (not severely restricted) — extreme calcium restriction is counterproductive as it raises oxalate absorption
Adequate dietary magnesium, which may reduce calcium crystallisation
avoid: Excessive caffeine and alcohol, which have diuretic effects that concentrate saliva
Very high dietary sodium, which raises salivary calcium excretion
Crash diets or prolonged fasting that drastically reduce oral intake and saliva production
Tobacco and nicotine products that reduce salivary flow volume

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate regular exercise is encouraged. During exercise, increase fluid intake proportionally to maintain salivary flow volume. There is no restriction on exercise related to sialolithiasis itself, though during an acute sialadenitis episode with fever, rest and hydration take priority until the infection settles. After sialendoscopy or intraoral surgery, gentle jaw movements are resumed as comfort allows, and contact sports that could injure the jaw are avoided for 2–4 weeks.
dailyManagement: Carry sugar-free sour candy to trigger saliva flow and reduce stasis throughout the day.
Note which meals or foods trigger swelling and bring this record to your specialist appointment.
Take any prescribed antibiotics for the full course; do not stop early even if swelling resolves.
After any stone removal procedure, attend follow-up imaging to confirm clearance and detect residual fragments.
Report new fever, rapidly worsening swelling, or difficulty swallowing promptly — these can indicate abscess formation requiring urgent drainage.
prognosis: The prognosis after successful stone removal is excellent for the majority of patients. When minimally invasive techniques achieve complete clearance, the affected gland regains normal function in most cases, with no long-term reduction in salivary output. Recurrence rates after sialendoscopy are approximately 5–18% over several years, mainly in patients with ductal strictures or metabolic risk factors. Conservative treatment without stone removal carries a higher recurrence rate and a greater risk of gland damage from repeated bouts of sialadenitis. Patients who develop chronic sialadenitis before treatment may have some lasting reduction in gland function, but this is rarely clinically significant given the output of the remaining glands. Submandibulectomy and parotidectomy are definitive but involve a small permanent risk of nerve injury. Overall, modern minimally invasive management has transformed the outlook, and open gland excision is now needed in a minority of patients.
recoveryTimeline: After successful sialendoscopy or intraoral stone extraction under local anaesthesia, most patients resume normal eating the same day or the next; mild duct oedema and a metallic taste can persist for 2–3 days. After ESWL, several treatment sessions spaced 1–2 weeks apart may be needed, with progressive reduction in stone size confirmed on imaging between sessions. Recovery after submandibulectomy under general anaesthesia involves 1–3 days in hospital, a further 1–2 weeks before resuming normal activities, and 4–6 weeks for full recovery. Sialadenitis without stone removal typically responds to antibiotics within 48–72 hours, but the obstructing stone remains and symptoms are likely to recur.
complications: Acute bacterial sialadenitis — ascending infection of the obstructed gland causing increasing pain, redness, fever, and sometimes purulent discharge from the duct; requires prompt antibiotics.
Salivary gland abscess — a collection of pus within the gland or surrounding tissues if sialadenitis is untreated; requires surgical drainage and intravenous antibiotics.
Ludwig's angina — rare but life-threatening descending infection from the submandibular space into the neck, causing floor-of-mouth swelling, airway compromise, and rapid systemic sepsis.
Chronic sialadenitis with progressive gland fibrosis — repeated bouts of inflammation replace functional acini with scar tissue, reducing long-term gland output.
Ductal stricture — scar tissue narrows the duct after inflammation or surgery, creating recurrent stasis and predisposing to new stone formation.
Nerve injury — marginal mandibular branch weakness after submandibulectomy or facial nerve palsy after parotidectomy; rates are low in experienced hands but represent the main serious surgical risk.

supportResources.name: NHS — Salivary Gland Stones
supportResources.description: Plain-English overview of salivary stones from the UK National Health Service, covering symptoms, causes, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery: Salivary Glands
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on salivary gland disorders including stones from a U.S. specialist professional body.

sources.title: Marchal F, Dulguerov P. Sialolithiasis Management: The State of the Art. Archives of Otolaryngology, 2003
Capaccio P et al. Modern Management of Obstructive Salivary Diseases. Acta Otorhinolaryngologica Italica, 2007
Koch M et al. Sialolithiasis: Epidemiology, Aetiology, Clinical and Histopathological Features and Treatment Outcomes. Journal of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery, 2010
NICE — Salivary Duct Calculi: Sialendoscopy (Interventional Procedure Guidance IPG321)
Zenk J et al. Sialolithiasis of the Major Salivary Glands. European Archives of Otorhinolaryngology, 2012
NHS — Salivary Gland Stones: Causes, Diagnosis and Treatment
Su YX et al. Sialendoscopy for Treating Obstructive Salivary Gland Diseases: A Systematic Review. British Journal of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery, 2009

confusedWithConditions.name: Dental abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dental abscesses produce jaw swelling from tooth-root infection and are associated with tooth pain and sensitivity, not the meal-triggered salivary colic and palpable duct stone of sialolithiasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Salivary gland tumour
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Salivary gland neoplasms cause painless, persistent gland swelling that does not fluctuate with meals, whereas sialolithiasis produces recurrent, meal-related, painful swelling that resolves between episodes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mumps
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mumps causes bilateral parotid swelling with systemic viral illness and fever without a palpable stone or meal-related fluctuation, whereas sialolithiasis is typically unilateral and triggered by eating.

relatedConditions.name: Salivary gland tumour
relatedConditions.relevance: Any persistent salivary gland mass requires imaging to distinguish a stone from a neoplasm; the two occasionally coexist.

relatedConditions.name: Sjögren syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Sjögren syndrome reduces salivary flow and alters composition, predisposing to stasis and stone formation; sialolithiasis can be the presenting complaint.

relatedConditions.name: Dental abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: Dental infections and sialolithiasis both cause jaw swelling and pain and must be distinguished clinically and radiologically, as management differs completely.

relatedConditions.name: Gout
relatedConditions.relevance: Hyperuricaemia in gout has been linked to salivary stone formation in several case series, and patients with recurrent sialolithiasis should have uric acid levels checked.

faqs.question: What causes a salivary gland stone?
faqs.answer: Salivary stones form when calcium salts crystallise around a tiny organic core inside the salivary duct, usually due to slow saliva flow, dehydration, or alkaline salivary pH. The submandibular gland is most affected because its duct runs upward against gravity and carries calcium-rich saliva.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have a salivary gland stone?
faqs.answer: The most reliable sign is a sharp, cramping pain and visible swelling beneath the jaw or in the cheek that starts within minutes of eating and slowly subsides over 30–60 minutes. You may also feel or see a small hard lump in the floor of your mouth. A doctor can confirm the diagnosis with ultrasound.

faqs.question: Can a salivary gland stone go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Small stones under about 3 mm, particularly those close to the duct opening, sometimes pass spontaneously with increased fluid intake, sour candy to stimulate saliva, and gentle gland massage. Stones larger than 3–4 mm rarely pass without intervention and usually require endoscopic or surgical removal.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for a salivary gland stone?
faqs.answer: For most patients, minimally invasive sialendoscopy — passing a tiny endoscope through the natural duct opening under local anaesthesia — removes the stone without an external cut and preserves gland function. Larger stones are fragmented first with a laser or shock-wave lithotripsy. Open surgery to remove the gland is now reserved for severe cases.

faqs.question: Is a salivary gland stone dangerous?
faqs.answer: Most salivary stones cause significant discomfort but are not life-threatening. The main risk is untreated obstruction leading to bacterial infection of the gland (sialadenitis). Rarely, infection spreads into the neck causing Ludwig's angina, a surgical emergency. Seeking treatment promptly avoids these complications.

faqs.question: Which salivary gland is most commonly affected?
faqs.answer: The submandibular gland, beneath the jaw, accounts for about 80–90% of salivary stones. Its long duct runs upward against gravity and produces calcium-rich, alkaline saliva — both features that favour stone formation. The parotid gland in the cheek accounts for most of the remaining cases.

faqs.question: How is a salivary gland stone removed?
faqs.answer: Options include sialendoscopy (endoscopic basket retrieval or laser fragmentation), extracorporeal shock-wave lithotripsy, simple intraoral incision for stones near the mouth opening, or, for large impacted stones, removal of the whole gland under general anaesthesia. The choice depends on stone size and location.

faqs.question: Do salivary stones come back after removal?
faqs.answer: Recurrence occurs in roughly 5–18% of patients over several years, mainly in those with ductal strictures, metabolic causes such as hypercalcaemia, or persistent dehydration. Addressing underlying risk factors and following up with imaging after removal significantly reduces the chance of recurrence.

faqs.question: Can dehydration cause salivary stones?
faqs.answer: Yes. Inadequate fluid intake concentrates saliva, raising mineral saturation and slowing ductal flow — both directly promoting stone formation. Drinking at least 2 litres of fluid daily is one of the most straightforward ways to reduce recurrence risk.

faqs.question: Will I need my salivary gland removed?
faqs.answer: Most patients do not need gland removal. Minimally invasive techniques resolve the problem in the majority of cases. Gland removal (submandibulectomy or parotidectomy) is reserved for chronically diseased, non-functioning glands or situations where endoscopic options have failed or are unavailable.

faqs.question: What happens if a salivary gland stone is not treated?
faqs.answer: Untreated obstruction causes recurrent painful swelling and eventually leads to chronic bacterial sialadenitis, which progressively damages gland tissue through scarring. Acute abscess or, rarely, spreading neck infection can develop. The gland may cease functioning normally over time, making early treatment worthwhile.

faqs.question: Can medications cause salivary gland stones?
faqs.answer: Yes. Drugs that reduce saliva production — including antihistamines, antidepressants, diuretics, and bladder medications — slow salivary flow and concentrate minerals, creating conditions favourable for stone formation. Reviewing your medication list with a doctor is worthwhile if you have recurrent stones.

faqs.question: Is sialolithiasis the same as salivary gland stones?
faqs.answer: Yes, sialolithiasis is simply the medical term for salivary gland stones. 'Sialon' means saliva in Greek, and 'lithos' means stone. The lay term and the medical term describe exactly the same condition.

faqs.question: What does salivary colic feel like?
faqs.answer: Salivary colic is a sharp cramping or pressure pain that builds rapidly under the jaw or in the cheek within 1–3 minutes of starting a meal — when saliva production surges against the blocked duct. The pain peaks within about 15–30 minutes, then gradually eases over an hour as the gland slowly drains.

faqs.question: Can a salivary gland stone cause a lump in the neck?
faqs.answer: Yes. Obstruction causes the affected gland to swell, which can appear as a tender lump under the jaw (submandibular gland) or in front of the ear (parotid gland). A hard nodule in the floor of the mouth may also be the stone itself. Any lump that does not follow the meal-related pattern should be assessed for other causes.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after sialendoscopy?
faqs.answer: Most patients return to normal eating within 24–48 hours after sialendoscopy under local anaesthesia. Mild duct soreness and a metallic taste typically resolve within 2–3 days. There is no external wound and no stitches with standard sialendoscopic stone removal.

faqs.question: Is shock-wave lithotripsy used for salivary stones?
faqs.answer: Yes. Extracorporeal shock-wave lithotripsy uses focused acoustic waves delivered from outside the body to break salivary stones into small fragments that can then pass spontaneously or be flushed out. It works best for parotid stones and submandibular stones under about 7 mm.

faqs.question: Can children get salivary gland stones?
faqs.answer: Sialolithiasis in children is rare. When it does occur it tends to be in teenagers and may be associated with metabolic conditions such as hypercalcaemia or hypercalciuria. Unexplained recurrent salivary gland swelling in a child warrants metabolic evaluation and specialist assessment.

faqs.question: When should I go to the emergency room for a salivary gland problem?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care if swelling rapidly spreads into the neck or floor of the mouth, if you have difficulty swallowing or breathing, or if you have a high fever with rigors and an increasingly red, fluctuant gland. These features may indicate Ludwig's angina or abscess — both require urgent surgical attention.

faqs.question: What doctor treats salivary gland stones?
faqs.answer: Oral and maxillofacial surgeons and ENT (ear, nose and throat) surgeons are the main specialists for sialolithiasis, particularly those trained in sialendoscopy. Initial assessment and antibiotic treatment of sialadenitis can be managed by a GP or general dentist, who then refers for specialist stone removal.

metaDescription: Salivary gland stones cause painful meal-time jaw swelling. Learn causes, diagnosis, and minimally invasive treatments including sialendoscopy. Doctor-reviewed.
